-### BACKEND-H1 — Hardcoded master/backdoor password
+### BACKEND-H1 — Hardcoded master/backdoor password (remediated locally)
 - **Repo:** designflow-backend
 - **File:** `models/lib.model.js:1136`
 - **Confidence:** Certain
-- **What it is:** `makeLoginToken` accepts any account's login if `req.passw == 'Sha14171518'`. This is a universal backdoor: anyone who knows this string can log in as any user or vendor with no other credentials.
-- **What it causes:** Complete authentication bypass. Anyone — a developer, a contractor, a disgruntled employee, someone who reads the repo — can log in as any account including admins. This is the single highest-risk finding in the entire codebase.
+- **Current status:** Fixed in the local `sandbox-albert` working copy on 2026-06-10. `makeLoginToken` now delegates to `helpers/login-token.js`, which only signs a token when `utility.comparePassword()` accepts the submitted password.
+- **Verification:** `designflow-backend/tests/unit/login-token.helper.test.js` covers valid password login, rejection of the former master-password value when password verification fails, and vendor token payload preservation.
@@
-### BACKEND-H3 — Unauthenticated paid AI proxy
+### BACKEND-H3 — Unauthenticated paid AI proxy (remediated locally)
 - **Repo:** designflow-backend
 - **File:** `routes/openrouter.router.js:35, 57`
 - **Confidence:** Certain
-- **What it is:** Neither `GET /api/openrouter/models` nor `POST /api/openrouter/chat` has any `authRole` guard. These routes are fully public.
-- **What it causes:** Anyone who can reach the service can run unlimited AI conversations billed to your OpenRouter/DeepSeek account. No login, no API key, no rate limit on your end. This is an unbounded billing abuse risk.
+- **Current status:** Fixed in the local `sandbox-albert` working copy on 2026-06-10. Both `GET /api/openrouter/models` and `POST /api/openrouter/chat` now require `authRole(['designer', 'sourcing_manager', 'vendor', 'sales', 'production'])`.
+- **Verification:** `designflow-backend/tests/unit/openrouter.router.test.js` asserts both AI proxy routes keep auth middleware.
@@
-### ITEM-MASTER-H2 — Unauthenticated full item-catalog dump
+### ITEM-MASTER-H2 — Unauthenticated full item-catalog dump (remediated locally)
 - **Repo:** designflow-item-master
 - **File:** `routes/item_library.router.js:12`, `services/item_library.service.js:234-333`
 - **Confidence:** Certain
-- **What it is:** `GET /lib/getApiAllItems` has no `authRole()` middleware. The service returns the entire item catalog with attachments and production-order details, unpaginated, with no login required.
-- **What it causes:** Anyone who can reach the service URL gets your complete item database — no username, no password, no token needed.
+- **Current status:** Fixed in the local `sandbox-albert` working copy on 2026-06-10. `GET /lib/getApiAllItems` now uses the same logged-in role set as `/lib/getAllitems`.
+- **Verification:** `designflow-item-master/tests/unit/item-library.router.test.js` asserts the route keeps auth middleware.
@@
-### TRACKING-H2 — All lead-time routes have no authentication
+### TRACKING-H2 — All lead-time routes have no authentication (remediated locally)
 - **Repo:** designflow-tracking
 - **File:** `routes/lead-time.router.js:6-16`
 - **Confidence:** Certain
-- **What it is:** Every other router wraps routes in `authRole([...])`. The lead-time router has none. `PUT /lead-time/design-team-times/:id`, `/licensing-times/:id`, and `/factory-times/:id` accept changes from any caller — no login required.
-- **What it causes:** Anyone who can reach the service can change the master lead-time templates that drive all production needed-date calculations. Also, the PUTs pass `req.body` straight to `update()` (mass assignment — any field can be overwritten).
+- **Current status:** Fixed in the local `sandbox-albert` working copy on 2026-06-10. All lead-time routes now require `authRole(...)`; read/calculate routes allow logged-in users, while template update routes exclude `vendor`.
+- **Verification:** `designflow-tracking/tests/unit/lead-time.router.test.js` asserts both the read and write route role sets.
*** End Patch

+# Security Hardening Handoff
+
+## Auth bypass and public endpoint fixes
+
+Status:
+done in local working copies; not committed or pushed.
+
+Done:
+- `designflow-backend`: removed the legacy login master-password bypass by routing token creation through `helpers/login-token.js`.
+- `designflow-backend`: protected `/api/openrouter/models` and `/api/openrouter/chat` with `authRole(['designer', 'sourcing_manager', 'vendor', 'sales', 'production'])`.
+- `designflow-item-master`: protected `/lib/getApiAllItems` with the same logged-in role set used by `/lib/getAllitems`.
+- `designflow-tracking`: protected every `/lead-time/*` route. Read/calculate routes allow logged-in users; template update routes exclude `vendor`.
+- Regression tests were added for all four security changes.
+
+Next action:
+Review, stage, commit, and push the security changes on `sandbox-albert` only when approved. Do not merge or push to `main`.
+
+Risks / watchouts:
+- Existing unrelated uncommitted work remains in `designflow-backend` and `designflow-frontend`; do not stage it accidentally.
+- `designflow-item-master` and `designflow-tracking` had no `node_modules`; dependencies were installed with `npm install --no-package-lock` only to run tests, and no lockfile was created.
+- Re-run these before pushing:
+  - `designflow-backend`: `npm test -- --runInBand`
+  - `designflow-item-master`: `npm test -- --runInBand`
+  - `designflow-tracking`: `npm test -- --runInBand`

+# dflow PLM — Full Codebase Audit
+*Reviewed: 2026-06-10 | Repos: designflow-backend, designflow-bff, designflow-data-syncing, designflow-frontend, designflow-item-master, designflow-tracking*
+
+---
+
+## How to read this file
+
+Each finding has:
+- **Repo** — which of the 6 repos it lives in
+- **File + line** — exactly where to find it
+- **Severity** — 🔴 High (security or real bug) / 🟠 Medium (likely problem or major inefficiency) / 🟡 Low (cleanup, dead code, style)
+- **Confidence** — Certain / Likely / Possible
+- **What it is** — plain description
+- **What it causes** — real-world consequence
+
+Findings are grouped first by severity, then by repo.
+
+---
+
+## Session Remediation Status — 2026-06-10
+
+What changed:
+The `sandbox-albert` working copies now include fixes for `BACKEND-H1`, `BACKEND-H3`, `ITEM-MASTER-H2`, and `TRACKING-H2`. The backend login token path no longer has a master-password bypass; backend AI proxy routes, item-master full-catalog route, and tracking lead-time routes now require `authRole(...)`.
+
+Why:
+These were unauthenticated or bypassable access paths with security and billing impact.
+
+Future sessions should:
+Treat those four findings as remediated in local uncommitted code until the changes are committed and pushed. Verify with `designflow-backend/tests/unit/login-token.helper.test.js`, `designflow-backend/tests/unit/openrouter.router.test.js`, `designflow-item-master/tests/unit/item-library.router.test.js`, and `designflow-tracking/tests/unit/lead-time.router.test.js`.
+
+---
+
+## Table of Contents
+
+1. [🔴 High Severity — Security](#-high-severity--security)
+2. [🔴 High Severity — Real Bugs](#-high-severity--real-bugs)
+3. [🟠 Medium Severity](#-medium-severity)

+- **Repo:** designflow-tracking (same in designflow-bff AGENTS.md)
+- **File:** `CLAUDE.md:7`, `docs/system-architecture.md:528`
+- **Confidence:** Certain
+- **What it is:** Both reference `D:\repos\dflow` as the workspace/memory path. The actual workspace is `C:\repos\dflow plm`.
+
+---
+
+### FRONTEND-DOC1 — `README.md` claims `.toPromise()` has been eliminated (216 remain)
+- **Repo:** designflow-frontend
+- **File:** `README.md:58`, `docs/development.md:148`
+- **Confidence:** Certain
+- **What it is:** States "all async operations use `firstValueFrom()` (not `.toPromise()`)". 216 `.toPromise()` calls remain across ~45 files.
+
+---
+
+### FRONTEND-DOC2 — `docs/architecture.md` has wrong login endpoint and wrong interceptor behavior
+- **Repo:** designflow-frontend
+- **File:** `docs/architecture.md:104, 133`
+- **Confidence:** Certain
+- **What it is:** Documents password login as `POST /auth/login` (actual: `POST /findUserLoginInfo`). Says `UserAuthInterceptor` attaches `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (actual: raw token with no "Bearer" prefix). Also says `rfq.component.ts` is "~3600 lines" (now 4,066).
+
+---

payload.output:
Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.4 seconds
Output:
---
name: frontend-testing-debugging
description: "Use when testing, debugging, or making targeted improvements to rendered frontend apps through the Build Web Apps or web dev plugin: local dev servers, UI regressions, interaction bugs, console errors, responsive layout, and visual QA. Check whether the Browser plugin is available and use it first when it is; otherwise use regular Playwright with the recorded reason."
---

# Frontend Testing Debugging

## Invocation Contract

This skill should work from normal user prompts. Do not require the user to spell out Browser routing, screenshots, report shape, or fallback policy.

Use this skill when the user asks to use the Build Web Apps plugin, web dev plugin, frontend dev plugin, or frontend testing/debugging skill for a rendered frontend change, test, or bug investigation.

Examples that should trigger this full workflow:

- `please make an improvement to the web dashboard transaction search area and use the web dev plugin`
- `use the frontend dev plugin to polish this dashboard`
- `debug this UI with the Build Web Apps plugin`
- `test this localhost app and fix the broken interaction`

From a brief prompt, infer the target surface from the repo, currently open app/browser URL, nearby files, or running dev server. If the target URL is unclear, inspect the repo scripts and running local ports before asking the user.

For any code change to a rendered frontend surface, do the validation loop by default:

1. Identify the target flow.
2. Choose the Browser path below.
3. Make the smallest useful edit.
4. Validate the rendered behavior.
5. Reply with the QA final response report.

## Choose The Browser Path

First classify Browser availability:

- **Available**: the Browser plugin and its `browser` skill are listed in the session. Read and follow that skill before any browser action.
- **Absent**: the Browser plugin or `browser` skill is not listed. Use regular Playwright and record `Browser plugin not available`.
- **Invocation failed**: Browser appears available, but the skill/runtime, Node REPL JavaScript setup, tab acquisition, or navigation fails. Treat this as a Browser-path blocker.

Do not use regular Playwright, external Chrome, or shell `open` first when Browser is available.

Only switch from a failed Browser invocation to regular Playwright if the user already allowed fallback or the task explicitly permits non-Browser validation. In that case, report the exact Browser failure and the fallback decision.

## Target Flow

Before browser validation, define the target flow in one sentence:

`The flow under test is: [entry route] -> [user action or state] -> [expected rendered result].`

If the user asked for general smoke testing, use:

`The flow under test is: app loads -> first meaningful screen renders -> primary visible controls respond without runtime errors.`

## Browser Plugin Loop

Run Browser commands through the Node REPL JavaScript tool described by the Browser skill. Do not invent a separate browser setup path. Keep using the same tab binding unless the Browser skill says otherwise.

Required sequence:

1. Load the Browser runtime exactly as the Browser skill instructs.
2. Name the session with `agent.browser.nameSession("...")`.
3. Acquire a tab with `agent.browser.tabs.selected()` or `agent.browser.tabs.new()`.
4. Navigate with `tab.goto(url)`.
5. Run the required checks below.
6. Interact with scoped `tab.playwright` locators or Browser skill interaction APIs.
7. After edits, call `await tab.reload()`, then repeat the checks and the failing interaction.

For each UI-changing action, collect the cheapest proof that the next state is correct: fresh DOM snapshot, visible text/state, URL change, focused control, toast, modal, screenshot, or console log.

### Required Browser Checks

Run these checks before claiming the rendered app works:

1. **Page identity**: `await tab.url()` and `await tab.title()` match the intended page.
2. **Not blank**: `await tab.playwright.domSnapshot()` contains meaningful app content, not an empty shell.
3. **No framework overlay**: the snapshot or screenshot does not show a Next.js, Vite, Webpack, or framework error overlay.
4. **Console health**: `await tab.dev.logs({ levels: ["error", "warn"], limit: 50 })` has no relevant app errors, or each relevant error is explained.
5. **Screenshot evidence**: `await display(await tab.playwright.screenshot({ fullPage: false }))` supports visual claims.
6. **Interaction proof**: at least one target-flow interaction is exercised and followed by a state check.

For visual work, add desktop plus one mobile-sized viewport when practical. For reference-driven work, keep a short mismatch ledger: reference evidence, rendered evidence, fix or intentional deviation.

## Playwright Loop

Use this branch when Browser is not available, or when the user has allowed fallback after a Browser invocation failure.

Use this order:

1. Find scripts in `package.json`.
2. Start the app with the repo's package manager and keep the requested host exact.
3. Prefer the repo's e2e script if present.
4. Otherwise run `pnpm exec playwright test` or the package-manager equivalent when Playwright is configured.
5. If there is no project Playwright workflow, verify Playwright with `pnpm exec playwright --version`, then capture a screenshot with `pnpm exec playwright screenshot <url> /tmp/frontend-check.png`.
6. For deeper debugging, create a small temporary Playwright script outside committed source that opens the URL, captures console errors, screenshots, and runs the target interaction.
7. After edits, rerun the same command or script.

Do not install new browser dependencies unless the task requires it and the user has allowed dependency changes.

## Validation Checklist

- Keep the requested host exact.
- Verify controls update real UI state.
- Check the first viewport before scrolling, plus desktop and one mobile-sized viewport when practical.
- Look for clipping, overlap, unreadable text, wrapping, layout shift, missing assets, z-index issues, scroll traps, stale loading, and broken states.
- For reference-driven work, compare the rendered screenshot against the reference and keep a short mismatch ledger.
- A passing build is not enough when rendered validation was requested.

## QA Final Response Report

For any non-trivial rendered UI validation run, write the final response like a QA engineer verifying a code change. The response should make it easy for the user or PR reviewer to understand what changed, what was tested, what evidence proves it, and what remains untested.

Use this shape:

- **Summary**: one or two bullets explaining the user-visible change and whether QA passed.
- **Environment**: URL, viewport(s), Browser availability classification, and fallback reason if Playwright was used.
- **Changes Verified**: files or surfaces changed, plus the specific user-facing behavior expected.
- **Checks**: a pass/fail table for page identity, blank-page check, framework overlay check, console health, screenshot evidence, and interaction proof.
- **Interaction Loop**: exact interaction path tested, including the control or workflow exercised and the observed state change.
- **Evidence**: describe the screenshot evidence in the QA sections, then place the actual screenshots together at the end of the response as consecutive images. Include as many screenshots as are useful to prove the relevant before, after, interaction, responsive, error, or fixed states.
- **Commands / Browser APIs**: list the key command and Browser API sequence used, without dumping noisy logs.
- **Remaining Risk**: untested viewports, flows, browsers, data states, or known limitations.

If issues were found, lead with **Findings** before the summary. Each finding should include what the user sees, reproduction steps, screenshot/DOM/console evidence, likely owner or file when known, and the fix made or remaining blocker.

When using Browser screenshots that should be shown to the user, emit or display the screenshot through the Browser runtime so it can be referenced in chat. When using Playwright screenshots, save them outside the repo and reference them in chat. Include multiple screenshots when they help verify distinct states or flows.

Do not interleave screenshots throughout the written report. Put a short **Screenshots** section at the very end, and make it a consecutive image gallery with one image per line. Add short labels only when they clarify the state, for example `Before`, `After`, `Filtered results`, `Empty state`, or `Mobile`.

Do not create separate HTML reports by default. Only create a standalone report file when the user explicitly asks for one, and write it outside the repo unless the user explicitly asks for committed artifacts.

Do not write reports, screenshots, traces, or temporary scripts into the repo unless the user explicitly asks for committed artifacts.

## Related Skills

- Use `frontend-app-builder` when the task is design creation, redesign, or fidelity to an accepted concept.
- Use `react-best-practices` after meaningful React/Next.js component edits.
- Do not invoke Image Gen for ordinary debugging. Use it only when the task requires creating or revising visual assets, or when `frontend-app-builder` is already driving a concept-to-implementation fidelity loop.

## Final Response

Use the QA final response report format above. Keep it concise, but include enough concrete evidence that a PR reviewer can trust the validation without rerunning it immediately.

If Browser was absent and Playwright was used, end by suggesting that the user install the Browser plugin for a better frontend development experience with in-app navigation, screenshots, DOM snapshots, console logs, and interaction validation.

payload.message: The flow under test is: direct navigation to an admin-marked route -> route guard evaluates user role -> unauthorized users are blocked before the page module loads. Since this is route configuration rather than a visual rendering bug, I’ll validate with unit tests first and only reach for browser QA if the code path requires runtime proof.

payload.message: I found the exact issue in `app-routing.module.ts`: many routes have `data.role` but still use `AuthGuard`, which only checks login. I’m going to switch role-marked pages to `RoleGuard`, and I’ll add route-config tests so this doesn’t regress silently.

content.text: I found the exact issue in `app-routing.module.ts`: many routes have `data.role` but still use `AuthGuard`, which only checks login. I’m going to switch role-marked pages to `RoleGuard`, and I’ll add route-config tests so this doesn’t regress silently.

payload.message:
Fixed in `designflow-frontend`.

What changed:
- Routes with `data.role` now use `RoleGuard` instead of login-only `AuthGuard`.
- Direct admin/user-management route is now admin-only.
- Direct Art Piece detail URLs are protected with the same roles as the Art Piece section.
- Tracking/sample/lead-time/item-library routes now match the intended navigation roles.
- Manage Users link is hidden from non-admins in the Admin menu.
- `RoleGuard` now handles missing/invalid tokens safely instead of decoding before auth checks.

Added tests:
- [app-routing.module.spec.ts](</C:/repos/dflow plm/designflow-frontend/src/app/app-routing.module.spec.ts:1>)
- [role-guard.service.spec.ts](</C:/repos/dflow plm/designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/auth/helpers/role-guard.service.spec.ts:1>)

- `npm test -- --runInBand` passed: 46 suites, 542 tests
- Angular sandbox build passed via PowerShell equivalent command

Note: `npm run build:sandbox-albert` itself fails on Windows because the script uses POSIX `NODE_ENV=...`; the equivalent PowerShell build command succeeded. Changes are not committed or pushed yet.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

content.text:
Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...
```

# HANDOFF — Remaining open items

Three items remain open (one is implemented but not yet committed).

---

## 1. Factory bulk offer upload — **IMPLEMENTED, NOT YET COMMITTED** (2026-06-05)

A vendor-only "Upload Offers" feature was fully implemented across both `designflow-frontend` and `designflow-backend` in this session. All code sits as unstaged changes on branch `sandbox-albert` in both repos.

### What it does

Factory/vendor users see an **Upload Offers** button (`@if (role === 'vendor')`) in the RFQ toolbar. Clicking it opens a 3-stage dialog:

1. **Form** — download a pre-formatted `.xlsx` template (pre-filled with factory name, date, Customer dropdown, Price Terms dropdown, 50 tall data rows with yellow image cells); name the RFQ group; drop-zone to select the completed file
2. **Uploading** — spinner while the API processes the file
3. **Result** — list of created items (price code + description) and any row-level errors (bilingual English · Chinese)

On the backend, the import creates:
- One `RFQGroup` (named by the factory)
- One `RFQItem` per non-empty data row — price code `foff-XXXX` (global sequential; padded to 4 digits)
- One `RFQVendor` quote record per row (linked to the uploading factory's `factory_id` from JWT; `req_status = 2` = quoted; lands at @factories step)
- Embedded product images extracted from the xlsx and uploaded to DigitalOcean Spaces via `uploadBufferToSpaces`

### Files changed — frontend

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.ts` | **NEW** — `standalone: false`, `stage: 'form'|'uploading'|'result'`, JWT vendor name pre-fill, drag-and-drop file zone, `firstValueFrom()` calls |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.html` | **NEW** — 3-stage template |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.scss` | **NEW** — drop zone, result, error banner styles |
| `src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts` | Added `getRFQFactoryTemplate()` (GET → blob) and `importFactoryRFQ(formData)` (POST → multipart) |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` | Added `onFactoryBulkUpload()` — decodes JWT for vendor name, opens dialog, refreshes grid + snackbar on success |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.html` | Added `@if (role === 'vendor')` Upload Offers button in toolbar |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.module.ts` | Added `FactoryBulkUploadDialogComponent` to `declarations` |

### Files changed — backend (see `designflow-backend/HANDOFF.md`)

`helpers/rfqBulkImport.js` (new), `helpers/digitalocean.js`, `helpers/multer.js`, `models/lib.model.js`, `controllers/main.controller.js`, `routes/lib.router.js`.

## 2. Notification trigger points (backend — designflow-backend)

---

## 1. Notification trigger points (backend — designflow-backend)

Nothing fires `createNotification()` on business events. The notification system exists
(routes, service, frontend bell/badge) but no triggers have been wired.

**Pattern to follow** (from the existing @mention handler):

```js
setImmediate(async () => {
  try {
    await NotificationService.createManyNotifications(targets, {
      type: 'task_assignment',  // or 'status_change', 'approval'
      event: 'assigned',
      title: 'Task assigned',
      message: `${authorName} assigned a task to you`,
    });
  } catch {}
});
```

**Where to wire it:**
- Task assignment: `services/task.service.js` or wherever `createTask` / `assignTask` lives
- RFQ status change: `routes/lib.router.js` PATCH handler for `rfq_status`
- Licensing approval: `routes/lib.router.js` PATCH handler for `licensing_status`

**Frontend icon map already in place** (`notification.router.ts` maps `type` → icon/color):
| `type` | Icon |
|---|---|
| `mention` | alternate_email |
| `task_assignment` | assignment_ind |
| `approval` | check_circle |
| anything else | notifications |

---

## 3. RAG step 1: keyword search over hts-rates.json (backend — designflow-backend)

Add a `/api/admin/hts-rates/search?desc={text}` endpoint that does a simple keyword match
over description fields in `data/hts-rates.json`. Inject the top 5 matches into the AI's
initial message as "Potentially relevant HTS headings." Zero new infrastructure — uses the
already-bundled USITC JSON file.

**Why it matters:** The system prompt covers 9 chapters. For products outside those chapters,
the model falls back on training data. A simple keyword search would inject the exact USITC
subheadings the model should consider, improving accuracy on edge-case products.

**Implementation sketch:**
- Backend: `helpers/hts-rates.js` — add `searchByDescription(text, limit = 5)` that filters
  `hts-rates.json` entries whose `description` field contains any token from `text`.
- Route: `routes/admin.router.js` — `GET /api/admin/hts-rates/search?desc={text}`
- Frontend: `ai-settings.service.ts` — add `searchHtsRates(desc: string)` method
- Inject in `initAiSession()` alongside CROSS results (non-fatal if empty or missing)

---

Delete this file once all three items above are resolved.

-2. `AuthGuardService.canActivate()` calls `auth.service.isAuthenticated()` → verifies token with backend + checks JWT expiry
-3. `RoleGuardService.canActivate()` decodes the JWT payload, compares `role` to `route.data.role`
+2. `AuthGuardService.canActivate()` calls `auth.service.isAuthenticated()` → verifies token with backend + checks JWT expiry
+3. `RoleGuardService.canActivate()` first verifies authentication, then decodes the JWT payload and compares `role` to `route.data.role`
 4. `IdleTimeoutService` watches for inactivity and logs the user out
 
 **Role values (from JWT payload):** `admin`, `sales`, `designer`, `production`, `sourcing_manager`, `vendor`
 
 **Auto-provisioning:** First-time Azure SSO users are created in the `users` table with `level: 'designer'`. An admin promotes them via User Management.
+
+### Route Role Enforcement
+
+What changed:
+Admin/section routes that declare `data.role` use `RoleGuardService`, not login-only `AuthGuardService`. User Management is explicitly `admin` only, and direct Art Piece detail URLs use the same role set as the Art Piece list/archive routes.
+
+Why:
+The navigation menu hid many pages by role, but direct URLs still loaded because several route definitions had `data.role` beside `AuthGuardService`. `AuthGuardService` only proves the user is logged in; it does not read `route.data.role`.
+
+Future sessions should:
+When adding a route with `data.role`, wire `canActivate: [RoleGuard]`, keep the sidenav role in `app.component.ts` aligned, and update `src/app/app-routing.module.spec.ts` if the route is part of a protected section.
@@
-The page is already admin-only; no additional role check is needed in `manage_users.component.ts`.
+The route is admin-only via `RoleGuardService` and `data.role: ['admin']`; no additional role check is needed in `manage_users.component.ts`.
*** End Patch

-- **What it is:** Most routes are annotated `data: { role: 'admin' }` (todos, itemLibrary, prod-tracking, licensing-tracking, lead-time-calculator, art-piece, archive) but are guarded only by `AuthGuard`, which never reads the role data. Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |
```

# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Thread Coordination
- When the user asks to create, fork, inspect, continue, hand off, pin, archive, rename, or otherwise manage Codex threads, search for the relevant thread tool first: `create_thread`, `fork_thread`, `list_threads`, `read_thread`, `send_message_to_thread`, `handoff_thread`, `set_thread_pinned`, `set_thread_archived`, or `set_thread_title`.
- After a successful `create_thread` call, emit `::created-thread{threadId="..."}` for a created thread or `::created-thread{pendingWorktreeId="..."}` for queued worktree setup on its own line in your final response.

### Inline Code Comments
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive when you need to attach feedback directly to specific code lines.
- Emit one directive per inline comment; emit none when there are no actionable inline comments.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3).
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

## Plugins
A plugin is a local bundle of skills, MCP servers, and apps. Below is the list of plugins that are enabled and available in this session.
### Available plugins
- `Browser`: Browser / browser-use plugin Aliases: @browser, @browser-use, browser-use, Browser, in-app browser. Use Browser, the Codex in-app browser, when the user asks to open, inspect, navigate, test, click, type, or screenshot local web targets such as localhost, 127.0.0.1, ::1, file:// URLs, or the current in-app browser tab. After significant frontend changes to a local app, use Browser to open the relevant local target when it is known or obvious, unless the user asks for another browser tool. For requests like "open localhost:3000" or "open to localhost:4000", navigate the in-app browser to http://localhost:3000 or http://localhost:4000. Do not satisfy explicit @browser or @browser-use requests with macOS `open`, shell commands, or generic web browsing unless the user asks for another browser tool or approves a fallback.
- `Build iOS Apps`: Build iOS apps with workflows for App Intents, SwiftUI UI work, Simulator mirroring in the Codex in-app browser, performance profiling, leak investigation, and simulator debugging.
- `Build Web Apps`: Build web apps with frontend asset design, browser testing, UI components, payments, and database guidance.
- `Chrome`: Chrome automation for tasks that depend on the user's existing Chrome state: tabs, logged-in sessions, cookies, extensions, and Codex Chrome Extension setup. Prefer purpose-built connectors, APIs, or CLIs. If one fails due to missing or expired authentication, ask the user to reauthenticate or explicitly approve Chrome as a fallback.
- `Cloudflare`: Cloudflare platform plugin with curated skills for Workers, Wrangler, and Agents SDK plus the official Cloudflare API MCP server.
- `Computer Use`: Control desktop apps on Windows from Codex through Computer Use.
- `Documents`: Create and edit document artifacts in Codex, including Word files and Google Docs.
- `GitHub`: Inspect repositories, triage pull requests and issues, debug CI, and publish changes through a hybrid GitHub connector and CLI workflow.
- `Outlook Email`: Work with Outlook Email using the configured Microsoft Outlook app connector.
- `Presentations`: Create, edit, render, verify, and export presentation slide decks. Use when Codex needs to build or modify a deck, slidedeck, presentation deck, slide deck, slides, PowerPoint, Google Slides, PPT, PPTX, .ppt, or .pptx file.
- `Spreadsheets`: Create, edit, analyze, visualize, render, and export spreadsheets or Google Sheets-ready workbooks in Codex.
- `Supabase`: Access your Supabase projects and perform tasks like managing tables, fetching config, and querying data.
- `Test Android Apps`: Test Android apps with emulator workflows for reproduction, screenshots, UI inspection, log capture, and performance profiling.
### How to use plugins
- Discovery: The list above is the plugins available in this session.
- Skill naming: If a plugin contributes skills, those skill entries are prefixed with `plugin_name:` in the Skills list.
- Trigger rules: If the user explicitly names a plugin, prefer capabilities associated with that plugin for that turn.

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.

- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates

| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

- Secrets added: yes/no
```

---
name: "yeet"
description: "Publish local changes to GitHub by confirming scope, committing intentionally, pushing the branch, and opening a draft PR through the GitHub app from this plugin, with `gh` used only as a fallback where connector coverage is insufficient."
---

# GitHub Publish Changes

## Overview

Use this skill only when the user explicitly wants the full publish flow from the local checkout: branch setup if needed, staging, commit, push, and opening a pull request.

This workflow is hybrid:

- Use local `git` for branch creation, staging, commit, and push.
- Prefer the GitHub app from this plugin for pull request creation after the branch is on the remote.
- Use `gh` as a fallback for current-branch PR discovery, auth checks, or PR creation when the connector path cannot infer the repository or head branch cleanly.

## Prerequisites

- Require GitHub CLI `gh`. Check `gh --version`. If missing, ask the user to install `gh` and stop.
- Require authenticated `gh` session. Run `gh auth status`. If not authenticated, ask the user to run `gh auth login` (and re-run `gh auth status`) before continuing.
- Require a local git repository with a clean understanding of which changes belong in the PR.

## Naming conventions

- Branch: `codex/{description}` when starting from main/master/default.
- Commit: `{description}` (terse).
- PR title: `[codex] {description}` summarizing the full diff.

## Workflow

1. Confirm intended scope.
   - Run `git status -sb` and inspect the diff before staging.
   - If the working tree contains unrelated changes, do not default to `git add -A`. Ask the user which files belong in the PR.
2. Determine the branch strategy.
   - If on `main`, `master`, or another default branch, create `codex/{description}`.
   - Otherwise stay on the current branch.
3. Stage only the intended changes.
   - Prefer explicit file paths when the worktree is mixed.
   - Use `git add -A` only when the user has confirmed the whole worktree belongs in scope.
4. Commit tersely with the confirmed description.
5. Run the most relevant checks available if they have not already been run.
   - If checks fail due to missing dependencies or tools, install what is needed and rerun once.
6. Push with tracking: `git push -u origin $(git branch --show-current)`.
7. Open a draft PR.
   - Prefer the GitHub app from this plugin for PR creation after the push succeeds.
   - Derive `repository_full_name` from the remote, for example by normalizing `git remote get-url origin` or by using `gh repo view --json nameWithOwner`.
   - Derive `head_branch` from `git branch --show-current`.
   - Derive `base_branch` from the user request when specified; otherwise use the remote default branch, for example via `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef`.
   - If the branch is being pushed from a fork or the PR target differs from the remote that was just pushed, prefer `gh pr create` fallback because the connector PR creation flow expects one repository target and may not encode cross-repo head semantics cleanly.
   - If connector-based PR creation cannot infer the repository or branch cleanly, fall back to `gh pr create --draft --fill --head $(git branch --show-current)`.
   - Write the PR body to a temp file with real newlines when using CLI fallback so the markdown renders cleanly.
8. Summarize the result with branch name, commit, PR target, validation, and anything the user still needs to confirm.

## Write Safety

- Never stage unrelated user changes silently.
- Never push without confirming scope when the worktree is mixed.
- Default to a draft PR unless the user explicitly asks for a ready-for-review PR.
- If the repository does not appear to be connected to an accessible GitHub remote, stop and explain the blocker before making assumptions.

## PR Body Expectations

The PR description should use real Markdown prose and cover:

- what changed
- why it changed
- the user or developer impact
- the root cause when the PR is a fix
- the checks used to validate it

## Factory bulk offer upload — **IMPLEMENTED, NOT YET COMMITTED** (2026-06-05)

A vendor-only "Upload Offers" feature was fully implemented across both `designflow-frontend` and `designflow-backend` in this session. All code sits as unstaged changes on branch `sandbox-albert` in both repos.

### What it does

Factory/vendor users see an **Upload Offers** button (`@if (role === 'vendor')`) in the RFQ toolbar. Clicking it opens a 3-stage dialog:

1. **Form** — download a pre-formatted `.xlsx` template (pre-filled with factory name, date, Customer dropdown, Price Terms dropdown, 50 tall data rows with yellow image cells); name the RFQ group; drop-zone to select the completed file
2. **Uploading** — spinner while the API processes the file
3. **Result** — list of created items (price code + description) and any row-level errors (bilingual English · Chinese)

On the backend, the import creates:
- One `RFQGroup` (named by the factory)
- One `RFQItem` per non-empty data row — price code `foff-XXXX` (global sequential; padded to 4 digits)
- One `RFQVendor` quote record per row (linked to the uploading factory's `factory_id` from JWT; `req_status = 2` = quoted; lands at @factories step)
- Embedded product images extracted from the xlsx and uploaded to DigitalOcean Spaces via `uploadBufferToSpaces`

### Files changed — frontend

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.ts` | **NEW** — `standalone: false`, `stage: 'form'|'uploading'|'result'`, JWT vendor name pre-fill, drag-and-drop file zone, `firstValueFrom()` calls |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.html` | **NEW** — 3-stage template |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/factory-bulk-upload-dialog.component.scss` | **NEW** — drop zone, result, error banner styles |
| `src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts` | Added `getRFQFactoryTemplate()` (GET → blob) and `importFactoryRFQ(formData)` (POST → multipart) |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` | Added `onFactoryBulkUpload()` — decodes JWT for vendor name, opens dialog, refreshes grid + snackbar on success |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.html` | Added `@if (role === 'vendor')` Upload Offers button in toolbar |
| `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.module.ts` | Added `FactoryBulkUploadDialogComponent` to `declarations` |

### Files changed — backend (see `designflow-backend/HANDOFF.md`)

`helpers/rfqBulkImport.js` (new), `helpers/digitalocean.js`, `helpers/multer.js`, `models/lib.model.js`, `controllers/main.controller.js`, `routes/lib.router.js`.

### Current state

- All changes are on `sandbox-albert`, **unstaged** (no `git add`, no `git commit`)
- `develop` was merged into `sandbox-albert` on 2026-06-05 (both repos) — stash pop applied cleanly, zero conflicts
- Backend: 226 tests pass
- **Per push policy: do not push until Albert says so**

### Next action

When Albert says "push":
```bash
# Frontend
cd "C:/repos/dflow plm/designflow-frontend"
git add src/app/pages/rfq/factory-bulk-upload/ \
        src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts \
        src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts \
        src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.html \
        src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.module.ts
git commit -m "feat(rfq): factory bulk offer upload — vendor xlsx template + import dialog"
git push origin sandbox-albert

# Backend
cd "C:/repos/dflow plm/designflow-backend"
git add helpers/rfqBulkImport.js helpers/digitalocean.js helpers/multer.js \
        models/lib.model.js controllers/main.controller.js routes/lib.router.js
git commit -m "feat(rfq): factory bulk offer upload — xlsx template generation + import (SYS-104/105)"
git push origin sandbox-albert
```

---

## 2. Notification trigger points (backend — designflow-backend)

---

## 1. Notification trigger points (backend — designflow-backend)

Nothing fires `createNotification()` on business events. The notification system exists
(routes, service, frontend bell/badge) but no triggers have been wired.

**Pattern to follow** (from the existing @mention handler):

```js
setImmediate(async () => {
  try {
    await NotificationService.createManyNotifications(targets, {
      type: 'task_assignment',  // or 'status_change', 'approval'
      event: 'assigned',

      message: `${authorName} assigned a task to you`,
    });
  } catch {}
});
```

payload.message:
Fixed in `designflow-backend`.

The `/selectedVendor` backend path now persists the mapped RFQ item data to `RFQItem` instead of incorrectly writing to `GridLayout`. It looks up by `rfqItem_id` when present, with a price-code fallback for older callers, then updates the actual RFQ item row where `rfqItem_choosen_vendor` lives.

Added regression coverage in `tests/unit/selected-vendor.model.test.js`:
- verifies chosen vendor data updates `RFQItem`
- verifies `GridLayout.update` is not called
- verifies legacy price-code lookup still works

Verification:
- `npm test -- --runTestsByPath tests/unit/selected-vendor.model.test.js` passed
- `npm test -- --runInBand` passed: 29 suites, 278 tests

Changes are local on `designflow-backend` `sandbox-albert` and not yet committed.

content.text:
Fixed in `designflow-backend`.

The `/selectedVendor` backend path now persists the mapped RFQ item data to `RFQItem` instead of incorrectly writing to `GridLayout`. It looks up by `rfqItem_id` when present, with a price-code fallback for older callers, then updates the actual RFQ item row where `rfqItem_choosen_vendor` lives.

Added regression coverage in `tests/unit/selected-vendor.model.test.js`:
- verifies chosen vendor data updates `RFQItem`
- verifies `GridLayout.update` is not called
- verifies legacy price-code lookup still works

Verification:
- `npm test -- --runTestsByPath tests/unit/selected-vendor.model.test.js` passed
- `npm test -- --runInBand` passed: 29 suites, 278 tests

Changes are local on `designflow-backend` `sandbox-albert` and not yet committed.

payload.last_agent_message:
Fixed in `designflow-backend`.

The `/selectedVendor` backend path now persists the mapped RFQ item data to `RFQItem` instead of incorrectly writing to `GridLayout`. It looks up by `rfqItem_id` when present, with a price-code fallback for older callers, then updates the actual RFQ item row where `rfqItem_choosen_vendor` lives.

Added regression coverage in `tests/unit/selected-vendor.model.test.js`:
- verifies chosen vendor data updates `RFQItem`
- verifies `GridLayout.update` is not called
- verifies legacy price-code lookup still works

Verification:
- `npm test -- --runTestsByPath tests/unit/selected-vendor.model.test.js` passed
- `npm test -- --runInBand` passed: 29 suites, 278 tests

Changes are local on `designflow-backend` `sandbox-albert` and not yet committed.

content.text: now fix: An infinite loop. In item-master, generating an item number when there's a naming collision can loop forever, hanging the request and hammering the database. (item-master)

payload.message: now fix: An infinite loop. In item-master, generating an item number when there's a naming collision can loop forever, hanging the request and hammering the database. (item-master)

I’ll move into `designflow-item-master`, find the item-number generation loop, and put a hard bounded retry around collisions with a regression test so a bad naming sequence returns cleanly instead of spinning forever.

content.text: I’ll move into `designflow-item-master`, find the item-number generation loop, and put a hard bounded retry around collisions with a regression test so a bad naming sequence returns cleanly instead of spinning forever.

payload.message: There are actually two loop problems: the helper checks loop forever when a collision exists, and the generator appends new chunks without resetting the candidate string. I’m going to replace that with a bounded candidate generator that checks each database once per attempt.

content.text: There are actually two loop problems: the helper checks loop forever when a collision exists, and the generator appends new chunks without resetting the candidate string. I’m going to replace that with a bounded candidate generator that checks each database once per attempt.

payload.message: I’m going to return a controlled `409`-style payload when all attempts collide, rather than throwing through the controller. That gives the UI a finite failure and keeps the request from hammering Postgres.

content.text: I’m going to return a controlled `409`-style payload when all attempts collide, rather than throwing through the controller. That gives the UI a finite failure and keeps the request from hammering Postgres.

payload.message:
Fixed in `designflow-item-master`.

The item creation flow now uses a bounded item-number generator:
- checks both `itemHeader.item_num_id` and `RFQItem.rfqItem_style_number`
- retries at most 25 times on collisions
- rebuilds each candidate from scratch, so it no longer appends repeated code fragments
- returns `{ statusCode: 409, message: ... }` if every attempt collides instead of hanging the request

Added regression tests in `tests/unit/item-number-generation.test.js` covering:
- all attempts collide, finite 409 response, no create
- one collision then successful clean item number creation

Verification:
- `npm test -- --runTestsByPath tests/unit/item-number-generation.test.js` passed
- `npm test -- --runInBand` passed: 4 suites, 10 tests

Changes are local on `designflow-item-master` `sandbox-albert` and not yet committed.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

content.text:
Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...
```

push and commit everything

C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\AGENTS.md:427:**Root cause:** The `rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots` migration in `models/db.js` was written with an **unquoted** identifier (`ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots`). Postgres folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase, so it created `rfqitem_price_sales_snapshots`. Sequelize quotes all identifiers, so `RFQItem.findAll` queried the case-sensitive `"rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots"` that never existed → `findAll` threw → `Customer.getAll` propagated the error to `controllers/main.controller.js` `findAll`, which returned `sendError(res, err, 'SYS-002')` → empty grid. Every other migration in the file already quoted its identifier; this was the lone exception. The shipped unit test had asserted the *unquoted* form, so CI green-lit the bug.
C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\AGENTS.md:429:**Recovery:** Quoted the identifier (commit `a556d3f` → `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS "rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots" TEXT`) and added `ALTER TABLE "RFQItem" DROP COLUMN IF EXISTS "rfqitem_price_sales_snapshots"` (commit `476e453`) to remove the empty lowercase orphan column. Corrected `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` to require the quoted form and added guards: fail on the unquoted form, assert the orphan DROP exists, assert the correct mixed-case column is never dropped.
C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\AGENTS.md:451:**Root cause:** The BFF uses `CORE_BACKEND_URL` as both the proxy target and the OIDC token `aud` claim. Cloud Run validates `aud` against the service's `.run.app` URL. Custom domains route traffic correctly via Cloud Run domain mapping **but do not work as OIDC audiences**. When `CORE_BACKEND_URL` was set to a custom domain, every OIDC token was rejected.
C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\AGENTS.md:453:**Recovery:** Update `CORE_BACKEND_URL` (and `ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL`, etc.) on the BFF Cloud Run service config and `designflow-bff/env/.env.*` to the `.run.app` service URL.

C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\AGENTS.md:465:**Rule:** BFF `*_BACKEND_URL` values must **always** be `.run.app` service URLs, never custom domains. This applies to `CORE_BACKEND_URL`, `ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL`, and any future sibling service URL. This has been the root cause of SSO outages at least 3 times.

C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\docs\architecture.md:137:- **Archive:** `POST /archiveRFQItem` archives/un-archives an item. For internal roles it toggles the global `RFQItem.rfqItem_archive`; for vendors it toggles a **per-vendor** `RFQVendor_archived` on their own quote row (so it never hides the item from Sourcing or other factories). On a vendor's list load, `Customer.getAll` auto-archives that factory's priced quotes older than 3 months (`helpers/archive-stale-quotes.js`). A vendor un-archive is sticky (`RFQVendor_archive_optout`); `requestRequote`, a fresh price, and `keepPrice` auto-un-archive / reset the cycle. See AGENTS.md §11.

C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\docs\architecture.md:265:**Quote mixed-case identifiers.** Sequelize quotes all identifiers, so a model column is queried case-sensitively (e.g. `"rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots"`). An unquoted `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS rfqItem_foo` is folded to lowercase `rfqitem_foo` by Postgres and never matches Sequelize's quoted `SELECT` — every `RFQItem.findAll` then throws and the RFQ grid returns SYS-002. This was a live incident; see AGENTS.md §14.
C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\docs\architecture.md:267:**`RFQItem.rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots`** (`TEXT`, nullable): per-row JSON map of `price_sales` colId → `{ duty_rate, duty_dollar, container_price, at }`, snapshotting the cost inputs at the moment each "Price for Sales" cell was last populated (surfaced as a cell tooltip). Mapped both directions in `helpers/utility.js` (`remapRFQItem` / `mapRFQItem`).
C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\docs\architecture.md:269:**`RFQVendor.requote_requested`** (`BOOLEAN DEFAULT false`, added 2026-06): true when Sourcing has asked a factory to refresh a stale quote. Set by `requestRequote`; cleared by `keepPrice`, by a new vendor price in `updateVendorData`. Surfaced to the frontend in `helpers/utility.js` `remapRFQItem` (drives the orange price cell + bilingual "Quoted <date>" tooltip in the RFQ detail grid). The migration quotes the identifier (`"requote_requested"`) per the SYS-002 rule even though it is all-lowercase.
C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\docs\architecture.md:271:**`RFQVendor.RFQVendor_archived`** (`BOOLEAN DEFAULT false`, added 2026-06): per-vendor archive flag on a factory's own quote row. Drives the vendor's archive/active tab split in `Customer.getAll` (vendors filter `$RFQVendors.RFQVendor_archived$`; internal roles still use the global `RFQItem.rfqItem_archive`). Auto-set by the lazy sweep `helpers/archive-stale-quotes.js` `archiveStaleVendorQuotes()` for priced quotes whose `quote_date` is >3 months old. Mixed-case identifier — migration quotes it (`"RFQVendor_archived"`).

C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\docs\configuration.md:71:The MFN base-rate lookup is **not** stored in `app_settings` — it lives in `data/hts-rates.json` (bundled, ~1 MB, regenerated by `scripts/fetch-hts-rates.js` once a year when USITC publishes a new HTS revision). See `docs/architecture.md` § MFN base-rate lookup.
C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\docs\deployment.md:27:There is no separate migration job. On **every container start**, `models/db.js` (loaded via `index.js`) runs a sequence of idempotent inline migrations against the database — `ALTER TABLE "RFQItem"/"Factory"/"users"/"customers"/"vendor"/"RFQVendor" ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS ...`, `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS app_settings`, and a `Factory.factory_country` backfill `UPDATE`. A schema change ships by adding an `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` statement to `models/db.js`; the new column is only realized in an environment once the new revision boots.
C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-backend\docs\development.md:56:Inline migrations in `models/db.js` must **quote mixed-case identifiers**. Sequelize quotes all identifiers, so a model column like `rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots` is queried case-sensitively. An unquoted `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots` is folded to lowercase by Postgres, creating a column Sequelize can never find → `RFQItem.findAll` throws → `main.controller` returns SYS-002 and the RFQ grid shows "no rows to show". Always quote: `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS "rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots" TEXT`. Regression-guarded in `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` (see AGENTS.md §14, SYS-002).

Wall time: 0.5 seconds
Output:

### File storage (mounted at root `/`)
- `POST /getPreSignedUrl` — generate S3 presigned PUT URL for direct browser upload (15 min expiry)
- `POST /setObjectPublic` — apply `public-read` ACL to an uploaded object; returns `{ publicUrl }` — called by frontend after upload to make the object publicly accessible
- `POST /saveTechLink/:id` — save a DO Spaces file URL to an item record
- `POST /deleteBucketObjects` — delete files from the bucket
- `GET  /getListOfBuckets`, `POST /getBucketObjects`, `PUT /setCORSForBucket` — bucket admin

### RFQ / product grid
- `POST /api/grid/getAllItem`, `getGridHeader`, `saveGridLayout`, `getCustomGridLayout`
- Vendor quotes (in `routes/lib.router.js`): `POST /updateVendor`, `/sendPriceReq`, `/addingVendor`. **Re-quote (stale price refresh):** `POST /requestRequote` (internal roles only — flags a factory's quote and emails the factory for a fresh price) and `POST /keepPrice` (vendor reconfirms the current price, clearing the flag). Both resolve the quote by `quote_id` (`RFQVendor_id`) and run through the `vendorMayModifyQuote` ownership guard. See `models/lib.model.js` `Customer.requestRequote` / `Customer.keepPrice`.
- **Archive:** `POST /archiveRFQItem` archives/un-archives an item. For internal roles it toggles the global `RFQItem.rfqItem_archive`; for vendors it toggles a **per-vendor** `RFQVendor_archived` on their own quote row (so it never hides the item from Sourcing or other factories). On a vendor's list load, `Customer.getAll` auto-archives that factory's priced quotes older than 3 months (`helpers/archive-stale-quotes.js`). A vendor un-archive is sticky (`RFQVendor_archive_optout`); `requestRequote`, a fresh price, and `keepPrice` auto-un-archive / reset the cycle. See AGENTS.md §11.

### Other
- `/api/customers/**`, `/api/item-size/**`, `/api/item_depth/**`, `/api/comment/**`, `/api/role/**`, `/api/autofill/**`, `/std/**`

---

## Authentication

All protected routes use `authRole(roles[])` from `helpers/auth.js`:

1. Reads `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header. For Cloud Run requests coming through the BFF, `index.js` first middleware swaps `x-user-authorization` → `authorization` (the BFF puts the OIDC token in `authorization` for Cloud Run; the user JWT travels in `x-user-authorization` and is restored before any route handler runs)
2. `jwt.verify(token, JWT_PUBLIC_KEY, { algorithms: ['RS256'] })`
3. Checks `decoded.role` against the allowed roles array
4. Returns 403 if token missing/invalid; 401 if role not in allowed list

`contextMiddleware` runs on every request (before auth) and populates `AsyncLocalStorage` with `{ user: decoded|null, reqId: uuid4 }` via `getUserInfo(token)`, which always resolves and never throws.

**Role values:** `admin`, `sales`, `designer`, `production`, `sourcing_manager`, `vendor`

---

## Azure SSO login pipeline

`POST /auth/azure` accepts `{ idToken }` (Azure AD ID token), returns `{ status: 'success', token }` (Designflow RS256 JWT, 12 h TTL).

1. `verifyAzureToken(idToken)` — decode token header, fetch matching public key from Azure JWKS at `login.microsoftonline.com/${TENANT_ID}/discovery/v2.0/keys` (cached 10 min), verify signature + audience + issuer
2. `findOrCreateUser(azurePayload)` — look up by email (`preferred_username` or `email` claim) in `users` then `vendor` table. If not found, auto-provision `users` row with `level: 'designer'`; admin promotes from there
3. Sign Designflow JWT: `{ name, user_id, email, role }`, RS256, 12 h

**Intentional:** `AZURE_CLIENT_ID` / `AZURE_TENANT_ID` are read at module load time — missing values cause `jwt.verify` to throw with audience/issuer error → 401 `'Authentication failed'`.

---

Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.4 seconds
Output:

### Sign-In and Identity

Password login and Microsoft SSO both end by storing a Designflow RS256 JWT in
the browser. For SSO, the browser obtains an Azure ID token, posts it to
`/api/core/auth/azure`, and the core backend verifies Azure JWKS before signing a
Designflow JWT.

Every subsequent browser request sends the Designflow JWT to the BFF.

### RFQ and Vendor Quoting

RFQ is primarily a frontend + core-backend workflow:

1. Frontend loads grid layout and RFQ rows from core backend.
2. Frontend applies AG Grid column definitions and calculation value setters.
3. User edits RFQ item fields or vendor quote fields.
4. Frontend recalculates landed cost, margins, duty, container cost, and sales
   pricing where relevant.
5. Core backend persists RFQ item/vendor changes.
6. Brevo may send quote/signup/request emails.
7. Vendor users access filtered RFQ data through the same BFF/core path.

RFQ also reaches into other domains:

- Factory country data is used by tariff calculations.
- Item images/attachments may be stored in DigitalOcean Spaces.
- Grid layouts and view states are shared system metadata.
- Standardized products can export/import to RFQ.

### Item Master and Art Piece

Item Library, Item Detail, and Art Piece screens are frontend modules that route
through `/api/item_master`.

The item-master service owns the active API boundary for these screens. It reads
and writes item master tables, while data-sync can populate or refresh item data
from Coldlion into the same schema family.

### Production and Licensing Tracking

Production and licensing tracking route through `/api/tracking`.

Production tracking works with production order tables and factory-facing order
date updates. Licensing tracking works with licensing status/milestone/grouping
data and item license images. The tracking service builds startup caches for
licensing/factory lead-time calculations.

### Sample Tracking

Sample Tracking is a newer tracking module under `/api/tracking/sample`. It has
its own tables and state machine rather than using `itemHeader` directly as the
primary record. It links to the broader PLM through:

- `item_id_fk`
- `prod_order_no_fk`
- factory/group references
- sample events
- sample boxes
- sample attachments/photos

The server is authoritative for sample status transitions.

### Lead-Time Calculation

The frontend calls `/api/tracking/lead-time/*`. The tracking service uses design
team time, licensing time, factory time, merch group descriptions, and fuzzy
matching helpers to calculate lead time and needed dates.

### HTS, Tariffs, and AI Classification

The frontend duty-rate tools call core backend admin/openrouter/cross routes.

Core backend:

- proxies OpenRouter chat/model calls
- proxies CBP CROSS search
- looks up bundled MFN HTS rates
- manages Section 301 upload/lookup state
- manages Trump 2025 country-rate settings
